segments.text:
Tetanus
CLS-N390 Vaccines: S c ience and Society

Clostridium tetani
A naerobic (no oxygen) Gram positive bacillus
C reates spores to survive
F ound all over – in dirt and dust
M ember of Clostridium genus
S ome species are not a problem, others cause gangreen ( C. perfringens ), botulism ( C. botulinum ), and diarrhea ( C. difficile )

Transmission
Spores enter through broken skin  - injury or contaminated items
More common routes of entry:
Wounds contaminated with dirt, poop, or spit
Wounds caused by puncture – nail or needle
Burns
Crush injury
Injury with dead tissue
Less common routes of entry:
Clean superficial wounds (only top layer of skin gone)
Surgical procedures
Insect bites
Dental infections
Compound fractures
Chronic sores and infections
IV drug users
Intramuscular injections

Tetanus
Incubation period – 3 – 21 days (10 day average)
May range from one day to several months
Most occur within 14 days
Produces two toxins – one is a neurotoxin
One of the most potent toxins known, based on weight
Lethal dose = 2.5 nanograms per kilogram of body weight
154 lb – 175 nanograms
1 nanogram = 1 billionth of a gram

Symptoms
Often referred to as lockjaw
Tightening of jaw muscle seen in 80% of cases
Jaw cramping
Sudden, involuntary muscle tightening (spasms) often in stomach
Painful muscle stiffness all over
Trouble swallowing
Seizures
Headache
Fever and sweating
Changes in blood pressure and heart rate

Complications
Serious health problems include:
Uncontrolled, involuntary tightening of vocal cords – laryngospasm
Broken bones
Infections while in hospital
Blockage of main artery of lung or branches by blood clots that has traveled from elsewhere in the body – pulmonary embolism
Pneumonia from breathing in foreign materials – aspiration pneumonia
Breathing difficulty, possibly leading to death – 1 to 2 in 10 cases

Intended to elicit anti-toxin antibodies Does not target bacteria
Vaccine

Tetanus toxoid first produced in 1924
Used extensively in servicemen during World War II
History of Tetanus Vaccine Use
70 cases in World War I
12 cases in World War II

Characteristics of Vaccine
Toxoid produced by growing bacteria in liquid media
Efficacy – approximately 100%; adsorbed is better
Immunity – approximately 10 years
Filtrate incubated with formaldehyde – toxin  toxoid
Adsorbed onto an aluminum salt OR liquid
<12 months 4 doses; 12+ months – 3 doses;
booster @ 4-6 years; booster @ 11-12 years

Vaccine Options
DT – pediatric diphtheria toxoid and tetanus toxoid
Given when child has pertussis contraindication
DTaP – pediatric diphtheria toxoid, tetanus toxoid, and acellular pertussis
<7 years old
Td – adult diphtheria toxoid and tetanus toxoid
Provided as booster shot every 10 years
Given if childhood series is missed and pertussis contraindication
Tdap – adult diphtheria toxoid, tetanus toxoid, and acellular pertussis
Given when childhood series is missed before 7
>7 years old
DTaP- HepB -IPV – combination of pediatric diphtheria toxoid, tetanus toxoid, acellular pertussis, hepatitis B, poliovirus
DTaP-IPV-Hib – combination of pediatric diphtheria toxoid, tetanus toxoid, acellular pertussis, poliovirus, and Haemophilus influenzae type B
Other

Tetanus in US
26 cases in 2019
Case numbers declined >95%  and deaths >99% since 1947
Sporadic cases in adults who don’t get vaccinated or stay up on booster
